skill.name: routeros-hotspot
skill.description: Guides RouterOS hotspot setup for captive portals, RADIUS authentication, walled gardens, DHCP option 114, and external login templates.
skill.summary: Configure RouterOS captive portals with hotspot profiles, RADIUS, walled gardens, and template variables.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis heavily overreported RouterOS CLI snippets and RouterOS $(...) template variables as shell or Ruby execution. No malicious code execution, obfuscation, prompt injection, or credential exfiltration was found, but the skill gives operational guidance for authentication systems and includes examples that can create insecure portal templates if copied without review.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped Template Variable In JavaScript Example
medium_findings.description: The login template example passes RouterOS variables into JavaScript string arguments. The reference notes that RouterOS provides no HTML escape variant and warns against placing user-controlled variables into script blocks without escaping.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example and the safety note are both present in the skill files. The practical exploitability depends on the external portal implementation and which variables can contain attacker-controlled text.

medium_findings.title: Authentication Examples Include Plaintext And Legacy Methods
medium_findings.description: The hotspot profile enables http-pap alongside HTTPS, and the reference describes plaintext password submission and md5.js for CHAP helper pages. These are legitimate RouterOS modes but can produce weak deployments if HTTPS is not enforced end to end.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The weak mechanisms are explicitly documented, but the skill also recommends HTTPS. This is a deployment hardening concern rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are RouterOS command examples or RouterOS server-side template placeholders. They are not local shell, Ruby backtick, or dynamic import execution by the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are Markdown documentation with RouterOS and HTML examples. I found no package code path that executes these snippets on the local host.

low_findings.title: Example Network Endpoints Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: Hardcoded IP addresses and URLs are example values for RouterOS, RADIUS, and MikroTik documentation. No evidence found that the skill contacts those endpoints directly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values appear inside documentation and code fences as examples. There is no runtime client, installer, or script that performs network access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw Captive Portal Variables In Script Context
dangerous_patterns.description: RouterOS template substitutions can reach JavaScript and HTML contexts. Generated templates should escape or avoid variables such as error text before using them in script blocks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The files include both the risky script-context pattern and the warning that no HTML escape variant exists. The issue affects generated portal templates, not the marketplace runtime.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Handling In Router Configuration Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes RADIUS secrets, certificate imports, login methods, and external authentication flow examples. These are expected for the domain but require careful secret handling and HTTPS enforcement.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Credential-related configuration appears intentionally and with some guardrails. Risk remains because users may copy examples into production with weak secrets or incomplete TLS setup.

content.value_statement: RouterOS hotspot setups are easy to break when firewall, DHCP, RADIUS, and templates interact. This skill gives focused guidance for captive portal configuration and common RouterOS traps.
actual_capabilities: Explains RouterOS hotspot chain behavior before regular firewall chains.
Drafts hotspot profile and instance settings for captive portal deployments.
Guides DHCP option 114 configuration for captive portal API discovery.
Documents walled garden patterns for HTTPS destinations and external auth hosts.
Summarizes RouterOS HTML template variables for login, status, and redirect pages.
Provides RADIUS client patterns, failover guidance, and secret management advice.
limitations: Does not validate commands against a live MikroTik router.
Does not replace a RouterOS security review before production deployment.
Focuses on IPv4 hotspot behavior because RouterOS hotspot is IPv4-only.
External captive portal examples need application-specific escaping and testing.

use_cases.title: Deploy A Guest Hotspot
use_cases.description: Create a RouterOS captive portal with HTTPS login, DHCP option 114, and controlled walled garden access.

use_cases.title: Integrate RADIUS Authentication
use_cases.description: Plan redundant RADIUS entries, delayed enablement, and template-based secret handling for customer hotspot deployments.

use_cases.title: Build External Login Templates
use_cases.description: Use RouterOS servlet pages and template variables when connecting an external authentication service.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Hotspot Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure a RouterOS hotspot for a guest VLAN. Include profile settings, instance settings, and common mistakes to avoid.

prompt_templates.title: Walled Garden Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my RouterOS hotspot walled garden plan for HTTPS external authentication hosts and asset domains.

prompt_templates.title: RADIUS Deployment Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RouterOS hotspot RADIUS client setup with two servers, delayed enablement, and secure secret handling.

prompt_templates.title: External Portal Template Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this RouterOS hotspot login template for unsafe variable use, redirect handling, and captive portal compatibility.

output_examples.input: I need a captive portal for a guest network with external authentication.
output_examples.output: A deployment outline covering hotspot profile choices, HTTPS certificate requirements, DHCP option 114, required walled garden hosts, and RADIUS considerations.

output_examples.input: My hotspot login page works, but phones do not detect the captive portal.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist for DHCP option 114 syntax, force behavior, CAPPORT probe timing, and api.json placement.

output_examples.input: Review this portal template before I upload it to RouterOS.
output_examples.output: A security review that flags raw template variables, redirect risks, missing escaped variants, and required external auth host access.

best_practices: Use HTTPS for login pages and test certificate names after import.
Use escaped RouterOS variables when placing values into URLs or query strings.
Tag generated walled garden entries with comments for safe repeatable updates.
anti_patterns: Do not block hotspot login traffic with regular firewall rules before understanding hotspot chains.
Do not hardcode production RADIUS secrets in reusable RouterOS scripts.
Do not place raw user-controlled template variables inside scripts or HTML attributes.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands on my router?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. You decide what to apply on RouterOS.

faq.question: Can it configure RADIUS for hotspot users?
faq.answer: It can draft RouterOS RADIUS client patterns and explain failover behavior. It does not manage your RADIUS server.

faq.question: Does it support IPv6 hotspot clients?
faq.answer: No. The skill documents that RouterOS hotspot relies on IPv4 NAT behavior.

faq.question: Why does it mention DHCP option 114?
faq.answer: Option 114 helps clients discover the captive portal API. The skill explains RouterOS syntax and timing issues.

faq.question: Can it help with external captive portal pages?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains RouterOS servlet pages, template variables, redirect flow, and escaping concerns.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: Use it as deployment guidance. Review generated commands, secrets, certificates, and templates before production rollout.
